title: Cpcb Epr Guidelines 2022
section: TITLE: CPCB Guidelines on Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)...
text: TITLE: CPCB Guidelines on Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) for Plastic Packaging — 2022 SOURCE: Central Pollution Control Board, India (Mock Policy Document for demonstration) SECTION 1: Overview of EPR Framework Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) is a policy approach under which producers are given a significant responsibility — financial and/or physical — for the treatment or disposal of post-consumer products. Under the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022), every Producer, Importer, and Brand Owner (PIBO) that introduces plastic packaging into the Indian market must register on the EPR portal maintained by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). SECTION 2: Registration Requirements All producers with an annual turnover exceeding INR 1 crore who use plastic packaging are required to register with CPCB. Registration must be completed on the centralized EPR portal at https://eprplastic.cpcb.gov.in. Upon registration, each entity receives a unique Producer ID (e.g., GREENPACK-001). Failure to register can result in penalties under the Environment Protection Act, 1986. SECTION 3: Categories of Plastic Packaging The Plastic Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2022, classify plastic packaging into the following categories for EPR purposes: - Category I (Rigid Plastic): Includes PET bottles, HDPE containers, plastic crates, and similar hard-shell packaging. - Category II (Flexible Plastic): Includes multi-use carry bags, pouches, sachets, wrappers, and similar soft packaging. - Category III (Multilayer Plastic / Multi-Material): Includes packaging made of more than one type of material such as multilayer packaging, tetra packs, and similar products. Producers must declare quantities separately for each category in kilograms (kg) on a monthly basis. SECTION 4: Monthly Declaration Requirements Every registered producer must submit a monthly declaration of the total plastic packaging (by category) introduced into the market during that month. The declaration must be submitted within 30 days of the end of each calendar month. Declarations are submitted via the CPCB EPR portal and must include: (a) Producer ID (b) Reporting month (YYYY-MM format) (c) Quantity in kilograms for each plastic category (d) Supporting procurement or sales records SECTION 5: EPR Targets and Obligations For FY 2024-25 and onwards, producers must ensure that the EPR obligation (percentage of plastic packaging that must be collected and recycled) is: - Rigid Plastic: 50% of total plastic packaging placed on the market - Flexible Plastic: 30% of total plastic packaging placed on the market - Multilayer Plastic: 30% of total plastic packaging placed on the market These targets increase progressively until 100% by FY

title: Cpcb Epr Guidelines 2022
section: category (d) Supporting procurement or sales records SECTION...
text: category (d) Supporting procurement or sales records SECTION 5: EPR Targets and Obligations For FY 2024-25 and onwards, producers must ensure that the EPR obligation (percentage of plastic packaging that must be collected and recycled) is: - Rigid Plastic: 50% of total plastic packaging placed on the market - Flexible Plastic: 30% of total plastic packaging placed on the market - Multilayer Plastic: 30% of total plastic packaging placed on the market These targets increase progressively until 100% by FY 2027-28. SECTION 6: Penalties for Non-Compliance Non-compliance with EPR obligations can attract: - Environmental Compensation as determined by CPCB - Cancellation of EPR registration - Legal action under the Environment Protection Act, 1986 - Public disclosure of defaulters on the CPCB website

title: Epr Certificate Trading 2023
section: TITLE: EPR Certificate Trading and Plastic Credit System...
text: TITLE: EPR Certificate Trading and Plastic Credit System — India 2023 SOURCE: CPCB Circular No. CPCB/EPR/2023/Plastic/001 (Mock Circular) SECTION 1: Introduction to EPR Certificates EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) certificates are tradeable instruments issued by the CPCB EPR portal to registered plastic waste processors (recyclers and co-processors) upon verification that a specified quantity of plastic waste has been collected and processed. One EPR certificate is equivalent to 1 metric tonne (1000 kg) of plastic waste processed. These certificates allow producers to demonstrate compliance with their EPR obligations without having to set up their own collection infrastructure. SECTION 2: Who Can Generate EPR Certificates EPR certificates can be generated by: - Registered plastic waste recyclers (mechanical and chemical) - Co-processors (cement kilns and other industrial facilities that use plastic waste as fuel) - Waste-to-energy plants approved by CPCB Recyclers must be registered on the CPCB EPR portal and must submit verifiable documentary evidence (weight slips, gate passes, consent orders) before certificates are issued. SECTION 3: Certificate Validity and Expiry EPR certificates issued in a given financial year (April 1 to March 31) are valid for that financial year plus the following two financial years. Certificates unused after 3 financial years expire and cannot be used to offset EPR obligations. Producers should maintain a certificate inventory and plan purchases to avoid certificate expiry. SECTION 4: Price Discovery and Trading Mechanism Certificate prices are determined by market forces on the CPCB portal. As of FY 2022-23, indicative prices ranged from INR 1,500 to INR 4,000 per metric tonne depending on plastic category and demand. Flexible plastic certificates typically trade at a premium because collection infrastructure for flexible plastic is less developed. Producers can buy certificates directly from recyclers through bilateral agreements or via the CPCB portal marketplace. SECTION 5: How to Purchase EPR Certificates Step 1: Log in to the CPCB EPR portal with your Producer ID. Step 2: Navigate to the "EPR Credit Marketplace." Step 3: Browse available certificates by plastic category. Step 4: Select the quantity needed to cover your EPR shortfall. Step 5: Complete payment through the portal's payment gateway. Step 6: Certificates are credited to your EPR account within 2 working days of payment confirmation. SECTION 6: Annual Reconciliation of EPR Obligations At the end of each financial year, CPCB reconciles each producer's EPR obligation (based on total declared quantities for the year) against certificates purchased and recycling done. If

section: Marketplace." Step 3: Browse available certificates by plastic...
text: Marketplace." Step 3: Browse available certificates by plastic category. Step 4: Select the quantity needed to cover your EPR shortfall. Step 5: Complete payment through the portal's payment gateway. Step 6: Certificates are credited to your EPR account within 2 working days of payment confirmation. SECTION 6: Annual Reconciliation of EPR Obligations At the end of each financial year, CPCB reconciles each producer's EPR obligation (based on total declared quantities for the year) against certificates purchased and recycling done. If a shortfall remains, CPCB issues an Environmental Compensation notice. The compensation amount is calculated as: Shortfall (tonnes) × Category-specific compensation rate (INR/tonne). Compensation rates are revised annually and published in the CPCB gazette notification. SECTION 7: Interaction with State Pollution Control Boards While CPCB oversees the national EPR portal, State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) have authority to conduct physical audits of producers within their jurisdiction. SPCBs may request original ERP records, procurement invoices, and reconciliation worksheets during audits. Producers must cooperate with SPCB inspections and provide records within 7 working days of a formal request.

title: Epr Enforcement Penalties
section: TITLE: EPR Enforcement and Penalty Framework for Plastic...
text: TITLE: EPR Enforcement and Penalty Framework for Plastic Packaging — India SOURCE: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change Advisory Note (Mock Advisory) SECTION 1: Regulatory Framework for Enforcement Enforcement of EPR obligations for plastic packaging in India is carried out under the Environment Protection Act, 1986 and the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022). CPCB and SPCBs are the primary enforcement bodies. They have powers to issue notices, impose environmental compensation, and cancel EPR registrations. SECTION 2: Types of Violations Common EPR violations include: (a) Failure to register on the CPCB EPR portal despite meeting the threshold turnover. (b) Non-submission of monthly declarations within the stipulated 30-day window. (c) Under-declaration of plastic quantities placed on the market. (d) Failure to meet annual EPR collection and recycling targets. (e) Submission of false or misleading data on the EPR portal. (f) Failure to cooperate with CPCB or SPCB audits. SECTION 3: Environmental Compensation Environmental Compensation (EC) is the primary financial penalty for EPR non-compliance. EC is calculated based on the shortfall in meeting EPR obligations: - EC for unmet EPR obligation = Shortfall (metric tonnes) × EC Rate (INR/tonne) - EC rates for FY 2023-24: Rigid Plastic: INR 15,000/tonne; Flexible Plastic: INR 20,000/tonne; Multilayer Plastic: INR 25,000/tonne EC notices are issued at the end of each financial year and must be paid within 60 days. Failure to pay attracts additional interest at 12% per annum. SECTION 4: Show Cause Notices Before imposing penalties, CPCB typically issues a Show Cause Notice (SCN) giving the producer 21 days to respond with reasons why penalties should not be imposed. Producers should submit detailed reconciliation reports, ERP data, and any corrective actions taken in response to an SCN. A well-documented response can result in reduction or waiver of penalties. SECTION 5: Registration Cancellation Repeated or willful violations can result in cancellation of EPR registration. Registration cancellation has serious consequences: - The producer cannot legally sell plastic packaging products under their brand. - The producer is listed on the CPCB website as a defaulter. - Criminal proceedings may be initiated under the Environment Protection Act. - The producer's bank accounts may be attached to recover outstanding Environmental Compensation. Registration cancellation is considered a last resort and is typically preceded by multiple notices and opportunities to remedy the violation. SECTION 6: Dispute Resolution Producers who disagree with a CPCB penalty order can: 1. File a representation

title: Epr Enforcement Penalties
section: sell plastic packaging products under their brand. -...
text: sell plastic packaging products under their brand. - The producer is listed on the CPCB website as a defaulter. - Criminal proceedings may be initiated under the Environment Protection Act. - The producer's bank accounts may be attached to recover outstanding Environmental Compensation. Registration cancellation is considered a last resort and is typically preceded by multiple notices and opportunities to remedy the violation. SECTION 6: Dispute Resolution Producers who disagree with a CPCB penalty order can: 1. File a representation with the CPCB Chairman within 30 days of the order. 2. Appeal to the National Green Tribunal (NGT) within 30 days of CPCB's final order. 3. Seek judicial review in the High Court in exceptional circumstances. The NGT is the preferred forum for EPR disputes and typically resolves cases within 6-12 months. SECTION 7: Good Compliance Practices To avoid penalties, producers should: - Set up automated monthly reconciliation between EPR declarations and ERP data. - Maintain a buffer of EPR certificates (at least 110% of projected monthly obligation). - Conduct half-yearly internal audits of plastic procurement and declaration data. - Designate a compliance officer responsible for EPR reporting and portal submissions. - Subscribe to CPCB notifications to stay updated on rule changes, EC rates, and portal updates.

title: Greenpack Reconciliation Handbook
section: TITLE: EPR Compliance Reconciliation and Audit Handbook —...
text: TITLE: EPR Compliance Reconciliation and Audit Handbook — GreenPack Internal Policy SOURCE: GreenPack Industries Internal Compliance Document (Mock Document) SECTION 1: Purpose of Reconciliation Every month, GreenPack Industries must reconcile its EPR declaration (quantities declared to CPCB) against its ERP procurement and sales records. This reconciliation ensures that the declared figures accurately reflect actual market introductions. A mismatch between declared and procured quantities may indicate: - Data entry errors in the ERP system - Timing differences (goods procured but not yet sold) - Undeclared stock movement - System synchronization issues SECTION 2: Tolerance Limits GreenPack's compliance policy defines a tolerance of plus or minus 5% between declared and procured quantities for each plastic category. Discrepancies within this tolerance are considered acceptable and attributed to rounding or minor timing differences. Discrepancies exceeding 5% in either direction must be investigated and, if necessary, corrected via a revised declaration on the CPCB portal. SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Reconciliation Process Step 1: Extract the monthly declaration data from the EPR portal for the relevant month and producer ID. Step 2: Extract the ERP procurement report for the same month, broken down by plastic category (rigid, flexible, multilayer). Step 3: For each category, compute the absolute difference and percentage difference: Diff% = ((Declared - Procured) / Procured) × 100. Step 4: Flag any category where abs(Diff%) > 5%. Step 5: For flagged categories, investigate the root cause and determine whether a revised declaration is needed. Step 6: Submit a revised declaration (if required) via the CPCB EPR portal within 7 days of the reconciliation date. Step 7: Document the reconciliation outcome and retain records for 5 years. SECTION 4: Revised Declaration Procedure To submit a revised declaration on the CPCB portal: 1. Log in with your Producer ID credentials. 2. Navigate to "Monthly Declarations" → select the relevant month. 3. Click "Revise Declaration" and update the quantities. 4. Upload supporting documentation (ERP report, reconciliation worksheet). 5. Submit for review — CPCB will approve or reject within 15 working days. SECTION 5: Consequences of Inaccurate Declarations Over-declaration (declaring more plastic than actually procured/sold) creates an inflated EPR obligation, leading to unnecessary expenditure on EPR certificates. Under-declaration (declaring less than actually put into market) constitutes a compliance violation and can attract Environmental Compensation notices from CPCB. Repeated violations may result in suspension of EPR registration, which would halt the producer's ability to legally sell plastic packaging products in

title: Greenpack Reconciliation Handbook
section: Submit for review — CPCB will approve or...
text: Submit for review — CPCB will approve or reject within 15 working days. SECTION 5: Consequences of Inaccurate Declarations Over-declaration (declaring more plastic than actually procured/sold) creates an inflated EPR obligation, leading to unnecessary expenditure on EPR certificates. Under-declaration (declaring less than actually put into market) constitutes a compliance violation and can attract Environmental Compensation notices from CPCB. Repeated violations may result in suspension of EPR registration, which would halt the producer's ability to legally sell plastic packaging products in India. SECTION 6: ERP Integration Best Practices To minimize reconciliation gaps, GreenPack should: - Configure ERP to auto-export plastic procurement data by category at month-end. - Map ERP SKUs to plastic categories (rigid/flexible/multilayer) using a master mapping table. - Schedule automated reconciliation runs on the 2nd working day of each month. - Set up alerts for discrepancies exceeding 3% (as an early warning before the 5% threshold).

title: Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022
section: TITLE: Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 — Amended...
text: TITLE: Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 — Amended 2022 (Summary) SOURCE: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), India (Mock Policy Summary) SECTION 1: Background and Scope The Plastic Waste Management Rules were first notified in 2016 and substantially amended in 2022 to introduce stricter Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations. The rules apply to every urban local body, gram panchayat, producer, importer, brand owner, plastic waste processor, and recycler involved in the plastic packaging supply chain in India. SECTION 2: Prohibition on Single-Use Plastics With effect from July 1, 2022, the manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use of identified single-use plastic items is prohibited. Prohibited items include: plastic sticks for balloons, candy sticks, ice-cream sticks, polystyrene (thermocol) for decoration, plastic plates, cups, glasses, cutlery, straw, trays, wrapping/packing films around sweet boxes, invitation cards, and cigarette packets, plastic or PVC banners less than 100 micron, stirrers. Exemptions exist for plastic packaging meeting minimum thickness requirements for rigid and flexible packaging. SECTION 3: Minimum Thickness Requirements Plastic carry bags must have a minimum thickness of 120 microns. Plastic sheets used for packaging must be at least 120 microns thick. These requirements are meant to ensure recyclability and reduce littering. SECTION 4: EPR Portal and Compliance The CPCB operates a centralized EPR portal for registration and compliance reporting. Producers, importers, and brand owners must: 1. Register on the portal and obtain a unique EPR ID. 2. Submit annual EPR action plans detailing how they will meet their collection and recycling targets. 3. Submit quarterly and annual returns showing actual collection and recycling achieved. 4. Purchase EPR certificates from registered recyclers to offset uncollected quantities. SECTION 5: EPR Credit System Producers can fulfill their EPR obligations by: (a) Setting up their own collection and recycling infrastructure. (b) Engaging plastic waste processors (PWPs) registered with CPCB. (c) Purchasing EPR certificates (credits) from registered recyclers on the CPCB EPR portal. Each certificate represents 1 metric tonne of plastic waste collected and recycled. The price of certificates is determined by market demand and supply on the portal. SECTION 6: Roles and Responsibilities Producer: Responsible for collecting, channelizing, and recycling plastic waste equivalent to their EPR target. Importer: Same obligations as a producer for all plastic packaging imported into India. Brand Owner: Any person who sells goods in India in plastic packaging under their brand name. Urban Local Body (ULB): Responsible for door-to-door collection of waste

section: metric tonne of plastic waste collected and recycled....
text: metric tonne of plastic waste collected and recycled. The price of certificates is determined by market demand and supply on the portal. SECTION 6: Roles and Responsibilities Producer: Responsible for collecting, channelizing, and recycling plastic waste equivalent to their EPR target. Importer: Same obligations as a producer for all plastic packaging imported into India. Brand Owner: Any person who sells goods in India in plastic packaging under their brand name. Urban Local Body (ULB): Responsible for door-to-door collection of waste and handing over plastic waste to registered recyclers. SECTION 7: Reporting and Record-Keeping All PIBOs must maintain records of: - Quantity of plastic packaging introduced into the market (by category) - Collection and recycling activities undertaken - EPR certificates purchased Records must be retained for a minimum of 5 years and made available for inspection on request by CPCB or State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs).
